revision.text:
[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's burial mound in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784年藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789年に崩御すると790年皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]
{{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Takano no Niigasa]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Takano no, Niigasa}} [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese nobility]] [[category:kk]][[category:790 deaths]] [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese concubines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Year of birth unknown]] [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese people of Korean descent]] [[category:kk]][[category:Emperor Kanmu]] [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese posthumous empresses]] [[category:kk]][[category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[category:kk]][[category:Yamato no Fuhito clan]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Takano no Niigasa]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Takano no Niigasa]]]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Takano no Niigasa]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] {{Japan-bio-stub}}

(1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784年藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789年に崩御すると790年皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->

(1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784年藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789年に崩御すると790年皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]

[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's burial mound in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784年藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789年に崩御すると790年皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784年藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789年に崩御すると790年皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's burial mound in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784年藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789年に崩御すると790年皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784年藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789年に崩御すると790年皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

[https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. [[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's burial mound in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784年藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789年に崩御すると790年皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's burial mound in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784年藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789年に崩御すると790年皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

(1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784年藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789年に崩御すると790年皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's burial mound in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784年藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。

It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784年藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789年に崩御すると790年皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->

It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784年藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789年に崩御すると790年皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790年皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]

revision.text:
[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]
{{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]
{{Japan-bio-stub}}

[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

(1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref><!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]

revision.text:
[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]
{{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]
{{Japan-bio-stub}}

<!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->

[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).

Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>

It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]

==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]

[[sn:Takano no Niigasa]] [[en:Takano no Niigasa]]
<!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->

revision.comment: Bot: mark page as missing translated-page template
revision.text:
[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]
{{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]
{{Japan-bio-stub}}

[[sn:Takano no Niigasa]] [[en:Takano no Niigasa]]
<!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->

Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

[[sn:Takano no Niigasa]] [[en:Takano no Niigasa]]
<!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Takano no Niigasa]] [[ja:高野新笠]] [[ko:다카노노 니가사]] [[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]] [[zh:高野新笠]]

[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[Concubinage|concubine]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]

==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had [[Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]

[[sn:Takano no Niigasa]] [[en:Takano no Niigasa]]
<!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->

<!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Takano no Niigasa]] [[ja:高野新笠]] [[ko:다카노노 니가사]] [[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]] [[zh:高野新笠]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[:en:Concubinage|concubine]] of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had [[:en:Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[Muryeong_of_Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]
{{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]
{{Japan-bio-stub}}

}}

Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

[[sn:Takano no Niigasa]] [[en:Takano no Niigasa]]
<!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->

[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[:en:Concubinage|concubine]] of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[:en:Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[Nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had [[:en:Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
[[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[:en:Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[:en:Concubinage|concubine]] of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[:en:Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[:en:Prince Sawara|Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had [[:en:Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]
{{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]
{{Japan-bio-stub}}
{{draft categories|
[[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:790 deaths]] [[Category:Japanese concubines]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Japanese people of Korean descent]] [[Category:Emperor Kanmu]] [[Category:Japanese posthumous empresses]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:Yamato no Fuhito clan]]
}}
[[sn:Takano no Niigasa]] [[en:Takano no Niigasa]]
<!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Takano no Niigasa]] [[ja:高野新笠]] [[ko:다카노노 니가사]] [[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]] [[zh:高野新笠]]
