revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=0720|death_date={{death date|0790|1|17}}|gender={{ill|female|qid=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|consort|qid=Q5784340}}|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|qid=Q595038}}|spouse={{ill|Kōnin|qid=Q958662}}|children={{ill|Emperor Kanmu|qid=Q314846}}; {{ill|Noto-naishinnō|qid=Q11612010}}; {{ill|Prince Sawara|qid=Q1079634}}|father={{ill|Yamato no Ototsugu|qid=Q26248568}}|mother={{ill|Haji no Maimo|qid=Q106160924}}|family={{ill|Yamato clan|qid=Q11417719}}|native_name=高野新笠}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q7677188]] Wikidata name: Takano no Niigasa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[:en:Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[:en:Concubinage|concubine]] of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[:en:Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[:en:Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[:en:Prince Sawara|Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had [[:en:Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]
{{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]
{{Japan-bio-stub}}

}}
[[en:Takano no Niigasa]]
<!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

[https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[:en:Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[:en:Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[:en:Prince Sawara|Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had [[:en:Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>

It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

[[en:Takano no Niigasa]]
<!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=0720|death_date={{death date|0790|1|17}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|consort|WD=Q5784340}}|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038}}|spouse={{ill|Kōnin|WD=Q958662}}|children={{ill|Emperor Kanmu|WD=Q314846}}; {{ill|Noto-naishinnō|WD=Q11612010}}; {{ill|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634}}|father={{ill|Yamato no Ototsugu|WD=Q26248568}}|mother={{ill|Haji no Maimo|WD=Q106160924}}|family={{ill|Yamato clan|WD=Q11417719}}|native_name=高野新笠}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7677188]] Wikidata name: Takano no Niigasa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[:en:Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[:en:Concubinage|concubine]] of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[:en:Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[:en:Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[:en:Prince Sawara|Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had [[:en:Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]

Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. Prince Junda, the second son of King [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[:en:Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[:en:Prince Sawara|Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had [[:en:Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]

{{Japan-bio-stub}}

}}
[[en:Takano no Niigasa]]
<!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。-->
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

[https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[:en:Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[:en:Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[:en:Prince Sawara|Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had [[:en:Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Takano no Niigasa]] [[ja:高野新笠]] [[ko:다카노노 니가사]] [[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]] [[zh:高野新笠]] {{wikidata link|Q7677188}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=0720|death_date={{death date|0790|1|17}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|consort|WD=Q5784340}}|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038}}|spouse={{ill|Kōnin|WD=Q958662}}|children={{ill|Emperor Kanmu|WD=Q314846}}; {{ill|Noto-naishinnō|WD=Q11612010}}; {{ill|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634}}|father={{ill|Yamato no Ototsugu|WD=Q26248568}}|mother={{ill|Haji no Maimo|WD=Q106160924}}|family={{ill|Yamato clan|WD=Q11417719}}|native_name=高野新笠}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7677188]] Wikidata name: Takano no Niigasa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, [[:en:Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyo Ward]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a [[:en:Concubinage|concubine]] of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]] and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[:en:Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[:en:Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[:en:Prince Sawara|Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had [[:en:Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).

December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[:en:Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).

Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]]. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>

[https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her [[:en:Japanese name|full name]] was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]] (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|Muryeong of Baekje]], was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be [[:en:Yamato no Fuhito clan|Yamato clan]]. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Prince Shirakabe]],<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]], and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and [[:en:Prince Sawara|Prince Sawara]] in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]] in 744. When [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]]. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown prince]] respectively, because of her [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had [[:en:Curse|cursed]] the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]] told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of [[:en:Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of [[:en:Prince Junda|Prince Junda]], son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]

The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>[[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=0720|death_date={{death date|0790|1|17}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|consort|WD=Q5784340}}|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038}}|spouse={{ill|Kōnin|WD=Q958662}}|children={{ill|Emperor Kanmu|WD=Q314846}}; {{ill|Noto-naishinnō|WD=Q11612010}}; {{ill|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634}}|father={{ill|Yamato no Ototsugu|WD=Q26248568}}|mother={{ill|Haji no Maimo|WD=Q106160924}}|family={{ill|Yamato clan|WD=Q11417719}}|native_name=高野新笠}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7677188]] Wikidata name: Takano no Niigasa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyo Ward}}, [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a {{ill|Concubinage|en|Concubinage|WD=Q188790|lt=concubine}} of {{ill|Emperor Kōnin|en|Emperor Kōnin|WD=Q958662|lt=Emperor Kōnin}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her {{ill|Japanese name|en|Japanese name|WD=Q877834|lt=full name}} was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of {{ill|Prince Junda|en|Prince Junda|WD=Q15113421|lt=Prince Junda}} (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King {{ill|Muryeong of Baekje|en|Muryeong of Baekje|WD=Q497878|lt=Muryeong of Baekje}}, was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be {{ill|Yamato no Fuhito clan|en|Yamato no Fuhito clan|WD=Q11417719|lt=Yamato clan}}. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of {{ill|Emperor Kōnin|en|Emperor Kōnin|WD=Q958662|lt=Prince Shirakabe}},<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji}}, and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and {{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara}} in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu}} in 744. When {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken}} died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as {{ill|Emperor Kōnin|en|Emperor Kōnin|WD=Q958662|lt=Emperor Kōnin}}. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|WD=Q207293|lt=Crown prince}} respectively, because of her [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had {{ill|Curse|en|Curse|WD=Q109411|lt=cursed}} the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>{{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|WD=Q332779|lt=Titsingh, Isaac}}. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|WD=Q37979|lt=Akihito}} told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of {{ill|Muryeong of Baekje|en|Muryeong of Baekje|WD=Q497878|lt=King Muryong}} of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of {{ill|Prince Junda|en|Prince Junda|WD=Q15113421|lt=Prince Junda}}, son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]
{{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]
{{Japan-bio-stub}}
[[en:Takano no Niigasa]]
<!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Takano no Niigasa]] [[ja:高野新笠]] [[ko:다카노노 니가사]] [[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]] [[zh:高野新笠]] {{wikidata link|Q7677188}} [[Category:790 deaths]] [[Category:Emperor Kanmu]] [[Category:Japanese concubines]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Japanese people of Korean descent]] [[Category:Japanese posthumous empresses]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato no Fuhito clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

[https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her {{ill|Japanese name|en|Japanese name|WD=Q877834|lt=full name}} was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=0720|death_date={{death date|0790|1|17}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|consort|WD=Q5784340}}|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038}}|spouse={{ill|Kōnin|WD=Q958662}}|children={{ill|Emperor Kanmu|WD=Q314846}}; {{ill|Noto-naishinnō|WD=Q11612010}}; {{ill|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634}}|father={{ill|Yamato no Ototsugu|WD=Q26248568}}|mother={{ill|Haji no Maimo|WD=Q106160924}}|family={{ill|Yamato clan|WD=Q11417719}}|native_name=高野新笠}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7677188]] Wikidata name: Takano no Niigasa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyo Ward}}, [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a {{ill|Concubinage|en|Concubinage|WD=Q188790|lt=concubine}} of {{ill|Emperor Kōnin|en|Emperor Kōnin|WD=Q958662|lt=Emperor Kōnin}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her {{ill|Japanese name|en|Japanese name|WD=Q877834|lt=full name}} was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of {{ill|Prince Junda|en|Prince Junda|WD=Q15113421|lt=Prince Junda}} (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King {{ill|Muryeong of Baekje|en|Muryeong of Baekje|WD=Q497878|lt=Muryeong of Baekje}}, was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be {{ill|Yamato no Fuhito clan|en|Yamato no Fuhito clan|WD=Q11417719|lt=Yamato clan}}. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of {{ill|Emperor Kōnin|en|Emperor Kōnin|WD=Q958662|lt=Prince Shirakabe}},<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji}}, and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and {{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara}} in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu}} in 744. When {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken}} died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as {{ill|Emperor Kōnin|en|Emperor Kōnin|WD=Q958662|lt=Emperor Kōnin}}. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|WD=Q207293|lt=Crown prince}} respectively, because of her [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had {{ill|Curse|en|Curse|WD=Q109411|lt=cursed}} the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>{{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|WD=Q332779|lt=Titsingh, Isaac}}. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|WD=Q37979|lt=Akihito}} told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of {{ill|Muryeong of Baekje|en|Muryeong of Baekje|WD=Q497878|lt=King Muryong}} of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of {{ill|Prince Junda|en|Prince Junda|WD=Q15113421|lt=Prince Junda}}, son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]

{{Japan-bio-stub}}

[[en:Takano no Niigasa]]
<!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Takano no Niigasa]] [[ja:高野新笠]] [[ko:다카노노 니가사]] [[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]] [[zh:高野新笠]] {{wikidata link|Q7677188}} [[Category:790 deaths]] [[Category:Emperor Kanmu]] [[Category:Japanese concubines]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Japanese people of Korean descent]] [[Category:Japanese posthumous empresses]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato no Fuhito clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=0720|death_date={{death date|0790|1|17}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|consort|WD=Q5784340}}|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038}}|spouse={{ill|Kōnin|WD=Q958662}}|children={{ill|Emperor Kanmu|WD=Q314846}}; {{ill|Noto-naishinnō|WD=Q11612010}}; {{ill|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634}}|father={{ill|Yamato no Ototsugu|WD=Q26248568}}|mother={{ill|Haji no Maimo|WD=Q106160924}}|family={{ill|Yamato clan|WD=Q11417719}}|native_name=高野新笠}} [[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyo Ward}}, [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a {{ill|Concubinage|en|Concubinage|WD=Q188790|lt=concubine}} of {{ill|Emperor Kōnin|en|Emperor Kōnin|WD=Q958662|lt=Emperor Kōnin}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her {{ill|Japanese name|en|Japanese name|WD=Q877834|lt=full name}} was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of {{ill|Prince Junda|en|Prince Junda|WD=Q15113421|lt=Prince Junda}} (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King {{ill|Muryeong of Baekje|en|Muryeong of Baekje|WD=Q497878|lt=Muryeong of Baekje}}, was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be {{ill|Yamato no Fuhito clan|en|Yamato no Fuhito clan|WD=Q11417719|lt=Yamato clan}}. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of {{ill|Emperor Kōnin|en|Emperor Kōnin|WD=Q958662|lt=Prince Shirakabe}},<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji}}, and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and {{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara}} in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu}} in 744. When {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken}} died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as {{ill|Emperor Kōnin|en|Emperor Kōnin|WD=Q958662|lt=Emperor Kōnin}}. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|WD=Q207293|lt=Crown prince}} respectively, because of her [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had {{ill|Curse|en|Curse|WD=Q109411|lt=cursed}} the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>{{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|WD=Q332779|lt=Titsingh, Isaac}}. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|WD=Q37979|lt=Akihito}} told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of {{ill|Muryeong of Baekje|en|Muryeong of Baekje|WD=Q497878|lt=King Muryong}} of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of {{ill|Prince Junda|en|Prince Junda|WD=Q15113421|lt=Prince Junda}}, son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]
{{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]
{{Japan-bio-stub}}

[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>

It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of {{ill|Prince Junda|en|Prince Junda|WD=Q15113421|lt=Prince Junda|1=Prince Junda}}, son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]
{{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]
{{Japan-bio-stub}}
[[en:Takano no Niigasa]]
<!--さらに山部親王の同母弟である早良親王も立太子された。早良親王は784th year 藤原種継事件に連座して淡路へ流される途中絶食して自ら命を絶った。藤原百川の陰謀であった。
新笠は桓武天皇の即位後、皇太夫人と称せられ、789th year に崩御すると790th year 皇太后、806年太皇太后を追贈された。新笠の墳墓は京都市西京区に高野新笠大枝陵（宮内庁管理）がある。--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Takano no Niigasa]] [[ja:高野新笠]] [[ko:다카노노 니가사]] [[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]] [[zh:高野新笠]] {{wikidata link|Q7677188}} [[Category:790 deaths]] [[Category:Emperor Kanmu]] [[Category:Japanese concubines]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Japanese people of Korean descent]] [[Category:Japanese posthumous empresses]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato no Fuhito clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takano no Niigasa]][[fr:Takano no Niigasa]][[ja:高野新笠]][[ko:다카노노 니가사]][[th:ทากาโนะ โนะ นีงาซะ]][[zh:高野新笠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=0720|death_date={{death date|0790|1|17}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|consort|WD=Q5784340|1=consort}}|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038|1=Empress of Japan}}|spouse={{ill|Kōnin|WD=Q958662|1=Emperor Kōnin}}|children={{ill|Emperor Kanmu|WD=Q314846|1=Emperor Kanmu}}; {{ill|Noto-naishinnō|WD=Q11612010|1=Noto-naishinnō}}; {{ill|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634|1=Prince Sawara}}|father={{ill|Yamato no Ototsugu|WD=Q26248568|1=Yamato no Ototsugu}}|mother={{ill|Haji no Maimo|WD=Q106160924|1=Haji no Maimo}}|family={{ill|Yamato clan|WD=Q11417719|1=Yamato no Fuhito clan}}|native_name=高野新笠}} [[File:Takano no Niigasa Burial.jpg|thumb|320x320px|Takano no Niigasa's Kofun in Oeda Kutsukakecho, {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyo Ward|1=Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto}}, [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Takano no Niigasa'''|高野 新笠||ca. 720 – 790}} was a {{ill|Concubinage|en|Concubinage|WD=Q188790|lt=concubine|1=Concubinage}} of {{ill|Emperor Kōnin|en|Emperor Kōnin|WD=Q958662|lt=Emperor Kōnin|1=Emperor Kōnin}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} and the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>Watts, Jonathan. [https://www.theguardian.com/world/2001/dec/28/japan.worlddispatch "The emperor's new roots: The Japanese emperor has finally laid to rest rumours that he has Korean blood, by admitting that it is true,"] ''The Guardian'' (London). December 28, 2001.</ref> Her {{ill|Japanese name|en|Japanese name|WD=Q877834|lt=full name|1=Japanese name}} was '''Takano no Asomi Niigasa''' ([[高野朝臣 新笠]]).
==Life== Niigasa was a daughter of [[Yamato no Ototsugu]] (和乙継) who was a descendant of {{ill|Prince Junda|en|Prince Junda|WD=Q15113421|lt=Prince Junda|1=Prince Junda}} (c. 480–513). Prince Junda, the second son of King {{ill|Muryeong of Baekje|en|Muryeong of Baekje|WD=Q497878|lt=Muryeong of Baekje|1=Muryeong of Baekje}}, was born in Japan and eventually became the ancestor of later known to be {{ill|Yamato no Fuhito clan|en|Yamato no Fuhito clan|WD=Q11417719|lt=Yamato clan|1=Yamato no Fuhito clan}}. Her mother was known to be [[Haji no Sukune Maimo]] (土師宿禰 真妹), whose surname is later, also known be [[Oe no Asomi]] (大枝朝臣). She became a concubine of {{ill|Emperor Kōnin|en|Emperor Kōnin|WD=Q958662|lt=Prince Shirakabe|1=Emperor Kōnin}},<ref>Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 86|page=86}}; Varley, p. 149.</ref> grandson of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}, and bore [[Emperor Kanmu|Prince Yamabe]] in 737 and {{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara|1=Prince Sawara}} in 750. Prince Shirakabe was married to [[Princess Inoe]], a daughter of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} in 744. When {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken|1=Empress Kōken}} died in 770, Shirakabe was appointed her successor and acceded to the throne as {{ill|Emperor Kōnin|en|Emperor Kōnin|WD=Q958662|lt=Emperor Kōnin|1=Emperor Kōnin}}. Princess Inoe and her son, Prince Osabe, were nominated as the Empress and the {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|WD=Q207293|lt=Crown prince|1=Crown prince}} respectively, because of her [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble birth]].
The sons of Niigasa had not been considered to be successors until 772, when the Empress (Inoe) was suddenly stripped of her Rank following accusations that she had {{ill|Curse|en|Curse|WD=Q109411|lt=cursed|1=Curse}} the Emperor. The crown prince, her son, was also disinherited. They were dead two years later. Subsequently, Niigasa's son Prince Yamabe<ref name="brown277">[https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 Brown, p. 277].</ref> was appointed as the crown prince and acceded to the throne as [[Emperor Kanmu]].<ref>{{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|WD=Q332779|lt=Titsingh, Isaac|1=Isaac Titsingh}}. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 86–95|page=86}}; Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA277 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 277–279]; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 148–150.</ref>
==Legacy== In 2001, Emperor {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|WD=Q37979|lt=Akihito|1=Akihito}} told reporters "I, on my part, feel a certain kinship with Korea, given the fact that it is recorded in the ''Chronicles of Japan'' that the mother of Emperor Kammu [Niigasa] was one of the descendant of {{ill|Muryeong of Baekje|en|Muryeong of Baekje|WD=Q497878|lt=King Muryong|1=Muryeong of Baekje}} of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]." It was the first time that a Japanese emperor publicly referred to Korean blood in the imperial line.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/japan/story/0,7369,625426,00.html Guardian.co.uk]</ref> According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Niigasa (720–790) is a descendant of {{ill|Prince Junda|en|Prince Junda|WD=Q15113421|lt=Prince Junda|1=Prince Junda}}, son of Muryeong, who died in Japan in 513 (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' Chapter 17).
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/03/03013.htm#003 Yamato (和) and Takano (高野) clans : the descendant of prince Junda, son of Muryeong of Paekche]
